message.content:
**The White Breath of the Hills**

The sky did not fall; it descended,
A slow, heavy exhale of gray wool,
Drifting down from the jagged teeth
Of the peaks that hold back the sun.
There was no wind to hurry it,
Only the hush of the afternoon,
When the world holds its breath
Waiting for the sky to remember how to weep.

First, a single flake,
A ghost against the darkening pines,
Then another, and another,
Until the air grew thick with diamond dust.
They landed on the slate roofs,
One by one, like letters
Unsent, unread,
Turning the brown shingles to ash.

Below, the village slept in the amber light,
Wood smoke curling from stone chimneys,
Thin blue ribbons seeking the clouds.
The cobblestones, usually rough with mud and hoof-print,
Began to soften,
Their edges blurring into white silence.
A bicycle leaned against a wall,
Its handlebars dusted with fresh powder,
A silent witness to the night’s arrival.

In the square, the fountain froze mid-splash,
Ice crystals forming on the spray like lace,
While the old church bell,
Rusted and heavy,
Swung once, a dull thud
Muffled by the accumulating weight.
The sound did not echo;
It was swallowed,
Absorbed by the growing depth.

Here, the snow does not cover;
It reveals.
It strips the chaos of the year—
The tire tracks, the scattered leaves,
The footprints of hurried steps—
And lays bare the skeleton of the land.
The mountains rise again,
Pure, terrible, and beautiful,
Their secrets locked in white.

By midnight, the
